---
title: "Ch 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable"
subtitle: "Balance the scales — what you do to one side, do to the other"
lesson-id: "cbse8.linear-equations-one-var"
tier: "cbse8"
ncert-class: 8
ncert-chapter: 2
ncert-chapter-title: "Linear Equations in One Variable"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["t1.intro-variables", "cbse8.rational-numbers"]
unlocks: ["cbse8.algebraic-expressions", "cbse8.factorisation", "cbse9.linear-equations-two-var"]
applied-domains: ["research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you read $3x + 5$ as "three times some unknown number, plus five"?
   If not, revisit
   **[Intro to Variables](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)**.
2. Are you comfortable adding $\tfrac{2}{3}$ and $\tfrac{1}{4}$? If not,
   revisit **[Rational Numbers](../01-rational-numbers/index.qmd)**.
3. If both sides of $2 = 2$ are multiplied by $5$, do you trust that the
   equation still holds?

If three were a confident yes, read on.
:::

## Touch

Imagine an old-fashioned two-pan balance. On the left pan: a known weight
of $7$ grams plus an unknown stone of weight $x$. On the right pan: a
known weight of $19$ grams. The pans hang level — they are balanced.

Move the slider. Watch the pans. Find the value of $x$ that makes them
hang level.

```{ojs}
viewof guess = Inputs.range([0, 25], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "Your guess for x (grams)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const left = guess + 7;
  const right = 19;
  const tilt = left === right ? 0 : (left > right ? -10 : 10);
  const status = left === right ? "BALANCED" : (left > right ? "left pan heavier" : "right pan heavier");
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;font-size:1.05rem;text-align:center;line-height:1.5;">
    <div style="margin-bottom:0.5rem;">
      <b>Left pan:</b> x + 7 = ${left} g &nbsp;&nbsp;|&nbsp;&nbsp;
      <b>Right pan:</b> 19 g
    </div>
    <div style="font-size:2rem;color:${left===right?"green":"#999"};transform:rotate(${tilt}deg);transition:0.3s;">
      &#x2696;
    </div>
    <div style="color:${left===right?"green":"#666"};margin-top:0.4rem;">
      ${status}
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

When you find the balance you have *solved* the puzzle. The arithmetic
behind the answer is the topic of this lesson.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. If both pans gain the *same* extra weight, do they remain balanced?
2. If both pans are halved (you remove half the weight from each), do
   they still hang level?
3. The puzzle on the slider had only one balance point. Could a similar
   puzzle have *two* answers — two different stone-weights that work?
4. Could a balanced equation have *no* answer at all?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice (don't peek until you've thought)

The balance is preserved if you do the *same thing* to both sides. That
is the only rule the chapter really uses. Whether the equation has one
answer, no answer, or many answers depends on its shape — we'll see all
three cases.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Undo addition

A box has $x$ apples. You put in $5$ more. Now there are $12$. How many
were there?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Write the situation as an equation, then *undo* the $+5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$x + 5 = 12$. Subtract $5$ from both sides.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x + 5 = 12 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 12 - 5 = 7$. Seven apples.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Undo multiplication

You have $3x$ rupees. You divide them equally among three friends; each
gets $24$ rupees. What is $x$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each friend got $\tfrac{3x}{3} = x$, but actually rethink: total $= 3x$, three friends, so each gets $x$. We're told each gets 24.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Each friend gets $x = 24$. Or read it differently: $3x = 72$, so
$x = 24$. (Multiplication is undone by division.)
</details>

### Problem 3 — Two operations, one variable

Solve $2x + 3 = 11$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$x$ is wrapped first by multiplying by $2$, then by adding $3$. Unwrap
in reverse order.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
First subtract $3$ from both sides; then divide by $2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2x + 3 = 11 \Rightarrow 2x = 8 \Rightarrow x = 4$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Variable on both sides

Solve $5x - 7 = 3x + 9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Get all $x$-terms on one side and all numbers on the other. Subtract
$3x$ from both sides first.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$5x - 3x - 7 = 9 \Rightarrow 2x - 7 = 9$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5x - 7 = 3x + 9 \Rightarrow 2x - 7 = 9 \Rightarrow 2x = 16 \Rightarrow
x = 8$. Check: $5(8)-7 = 33$ and $3(8)+9 = 33$. Balanced.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Brackets and fractions

Solve $\dfrac{x+1}{2} = \dfrac{x-3}{3}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Clear the denominators by multiplying both sides by $6$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$3(x+1) = 2(x-3)$, then expand.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Multiply both sides by $6$:
$3(x+1) = 2(x-3)$
$\Rightarrow 3x + 3 = 2x - 6$
$\Rightarrow x = -9$.
Check: LHS $= \tfrac{-9+1}{2} = -4$; RHS $= \tfrac{-9-3}{3} = -4$. ✓
</details>

### Problem 6 — Word puzzle

A father is currently four times as old as his son. Twenty years from
now, he will be only twice as old. Find their present ages.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let the son's age now be $x$. Then the father's age is $4x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
In 20 years: son is $x+20$, father is $4x+20$. The condition
$4x+20 = 2(x+20)$ gives the equation.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4x + 20 = 2(x+20) = 2x + 40 \Rightarrow 2x = 20 \Rightarrow x = 10$.
**Son is 10, father is 40.** Check 20 years later: father 60, son 30 —
yes, exactly twice.
</details>

You have just solved equations by always doing the *same operation* on
both sides until $x$ stood alone. That recipe is the whole chapter.

::: {.fun-fact}
The word **algebra** comes from the title of the 9th-century textbook
*al-Kitāb al-mukhtaṣar fī ḥisāb al-jabr wal-muqābala* by the Persian
scholar **al-Khwārizmī** (c. 825 CE). The phrase *al-jabr* literally means
"the restoring" — the act of moving a term from one side of an equation
to the other and reversing its sign. So when you "transpose $-3$ across
$=$ and write $+3$", you are performing al-Khwārizmī's *al-jabr* —
twelve hundred years on, and still doing the same dance.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A linear equation is a **balance**, not a sentence to be parsed. The
equals sign means *the two sides weigh the same*. Every step you take
is allowed for one reason only: it preserves the balance. Once you see
this, you stop memorising "transposition rules" and just do the same
thing to both sides — and the unknown drifts towards solitude on its
own.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **linear equation in one variable** is an equation of the form
$$ax + b = cx + d$$
where $a, b, c, d$ are rational numbers and $a \neq c$. It has exactly
**one solution**, found by isolating $x$.

The **rule of balance:** if $A = B$, then for any rational $k$,
$A + k = B + k$, $A - k = B - k$, $kA = kB$, and (if $k \neq 0$)
$A/k = B/k$.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Move terms across with the sign reversed;*
> *Same act on both sides keeps the truth.*
> *When at last the unknown stands alone —*
> *Read it off; that is the proof.*

The standard recipe:

1. Clear fractions (multiply both sides by the LCM of denominators).
2. Open brackets (distributive law).
3. Move all $x$-terms to one side, all constants to the other.
4. Divide by the coefficient of $x$.
5. **Always verify** by substituting back.

## Apply

### Worked example

> The sum of three consecutive integers is $72$. Find them.

Competent solver's path:

1. Let the smallest integer be $x$. The next two are $x+1$ and $x+2$.
2. Sum: $x + (x+1) + (x+2) = 72$.
3. Combine: $3x + 3 = 72$.
4. Subtract $3$: $3x = 69$.
5. Divide by $3$: $x = 23$.

The integers are $23, 24, 25$. Check: $23 + 24 + 25 = 72$. ✓

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Solve $7x = 42$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = 42 / 7 = 6$.
</details>

**W2.** Solve $x - 9 = -3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Add $9$ to both sides: $x = 6$.
</details>

**W3.** Solve $\tfrac{x}{4} = -2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply both sides by $4$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = -8$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Solve $4x + 9 = 25 - 2x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Get all $x$ on the left. Add $2x$ to both sides.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$6x + 9 = 25 \Rightarrow 6x = 16 \Rightarrow x = \tfrac{8}{3}$.
</details>

**P2.** Solve $5(x-2) = 3(x+4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Open the brackets first.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5x - 10 = 3x + 12 \Rightarrow 2x = 22 \Rightarrow x = 11$.
</details>

**P3.** Solve $\dfrac{2x-3}{4} - \dfrac{x+1}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
LCM of denominators is $12$. Multiply through.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Multiply by $12$: $3(2x-3) - 2(x+1) = 6 \Rightarrow 6x - 9 - 2x - 2 = 6
\Rightarrow 4x - 11 = 6 \Rightarrow 4x = 17 \Rightarrow x = \tfrac{17}{4}$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A two-digit number has digit-sum $9$. When the digits are
swapped, the new number is $27$ greater than the original. Find the
number.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let the tens digit be $t$ and the units digit be $u$. The number is
$10t + u$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$t + u = 9$ and $10u + t = 10t + u + 27$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
From the second equation: $9u - 9t = 27 \Rightarrow u - t = 3$.
Combined with $t + u = 9$: add to get $2u = 12 \Rightarrow u = 6$ and
$t = 3$. Original number: **36**. Check: swap gives $63$, which is
$36 + 27$. ✓
</details>

**S2.** Distance from town A to town B is travelled by a car at
$60$ km/h, and back at $40$ km/h. The total trip takes $5$ hours. Find
the one-way distance.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Time = distance ÷ speed. Let one-way distance be $d$ km.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$\tfrac{d}{60} + \tfrac{d}{40} = 5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
LCM(60, 40) = 120. Multiply through:
$2d + 3d = 600 \Rightarrow 5d = 600 \Rightarrow d = 120$ km. **One-way
distance is 120 km.**
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** The solution of $3(x-1) = 2x + 5$ is
(a) $5$ (b) $-2$ (c) $8$ (d) $2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Open the bracket, collect terms.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3x - 3 = 2x + 5 \Rightarrow x = 8$. **Answer: (c).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Solve and verify: $7y + 4 = -4y - 18$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Add $4y$ to both sides; subtract $4$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7y + 4y = -18 - 4 \Rightarrow 11y = -22 \Rightarrow y = -2$.
Check: LHS $= 7(-2)+4 = -10$; RHS $= -4(-2) - 18 = 8 - 18 = -10$. ✓
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Solve
$\dfrac{3x+1}{16} + \dfrac{2x-3}{7} = \dfrac{x+3}{8} + \dfrac{3x-1}{14}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
LCM of $16, 7, 8, 14$ is $112$. Multiply through.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
After multiplying: $7(3x+1) + 16(2x-3) = 14(x+3) + 8(3x-1)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7(3x+1) + 16(2x-3) = 14(x+3) + 8(3x-1)$
$21x + 7 + 32x - 48 = 14x + 42 + 24x - 8$
$53x - 41 = 38x + 34$
$15x = 75$
$\boxed{x = 5}$.

Check: LHS $= \tfrac{16}{16} + \tfrac{7}{7} = 1 + 1 = 2$;
RHS $= \tfrac{8}{8} + \tfrac{14}{14} = 2$. ✓
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** Two pipes can together fill a tank in
$12$ minutes. Pipe A alone takes $20$ minutes. (i) How long would pipe B
alone take? (ii) If pipe A is shut after $5$ minutes, how much longer
will pipe B alone take to finish the tank?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Think in *rates*. A fills at $\tfrac{1}{20}$ tank/min. Together they
fill at $\tfrac{1}{12}$ tank/min.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Let B's rate be $\tfrac{1}{x}$. Equation: $\tfrac{1}{20} + \tfrac{1}{x} =
\tfrac{1}{12}$. Solve for $x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** $\tfrac{1}{20} + \tfrac{1}{x} = \tfrac{1}{12}$
$\Rightarrow \tfrac{1}{x} = \tfrac{1}{12} - \tfrac{1}{20} = \tfrac{5 - 3}{60} = \tfrac{2}{60} = \tfrac{1}{30}$.

So pipe B alone takes **30 minutes**.

**(ii)** In 5 minutes both pipes together fill $5 \times \tfrac{1}{12} =
\tfrac{5}{12}$ of the tank.

Remaining: $1 - \tfrac{5}{12} = \tfrac{7}{12}$.

Time for B alone to finish $\tfrac{7}{12}$ of the tank at rate
$\tfrac{1}{30}$ per min: $\tfrac{7}{12} \div \tfrac{1}{30} =
\tfrac{7}{12} \times 30 = \tfrac{210}{12} = 17.5$ minutes.

**Pipe B needs another 17.5 minutes (= 17 min 30 sec).**
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Problem 2.2 (work-rate puzzles) is
a near-twin of the 5-mark item.
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2020 Set 2, Q9 ("solve the equation with
fractions on both sides") matches the 3-mark item closely.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Transposing means flipping the sign."** It means *moving and
   reversing*. When you move $+3$ across $=$, it becomes $-3$. The reason
   is that you are subtracting $3$ from both sides — the sign reversal is
   a *consequence* of the balance rule, not a magic move.
2. **"You can multiply both sides by $x$ to clear it."** Sometimes — but
   if $x$ might be $0$ you can lose solutions or invent fake ones.
   Always pause when you multiply by something containing the unknown.
3. **"The check is optional."** It is not. A wrong sign somewhere will
   pass undetected unless you substitute back and read both sides.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [linear equation](/glossary.qmd#linear-equation) in one variable
   has the form $ax + b = cx + d$ — and exactly one
   [solution](/glossary.qmd#solution-of-an-equation), found by
   isolating $x$.**
2. **Whatever you do to one side of an
   [equation](/glossary.qmd#equation), do to the other. Add, subtract,
   multiply (by non-zero), divide (by non-zero) — the balance rule is
   the only rule the chapter uses.**
3. **Always substitute the answer back. The check is not optional —
   it is the proof that your moves preserved the balance.**

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you explain to a friend, without notes, *why* moving a term
   across the $=$ sign reverses its sign?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   *Practice* problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you turn a one-paragraph word problem into a linear equation?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Understanding Quadrilaterals](../03-understanding-quadrilaterals/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo *Stretch S2* and the 5-mark board item, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** rebuild from *Discover*. Most stumbles here are about
  fraction handling — revisit
  **[Rational Numbers](../01-rational-numbers/index.qmd)**.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Intro to Variables](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)** — letters as placeholders for unknown numbers.
- **[Rational Numbers](../01-rational-numbers/index.qmd)** — solutions live in $\mathbb{Q}$.

### What this unlocks

- **[Algebraic Expressions and Identities](../08-algebraic-expressions/index.qmd)** — manipulating expressions before solving them.
- **[Factorisation](../12-factorisation/index.qmd)** — equations whose left side is a product of two factors.
- **[Class 9 — Linear Equations in Two Variables](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/04-linear-equations-two-vars/index.qmd)** — adds a second unknown.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 8, Chapter 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 2.1–2.4: solving equations
  with the variable on either or both sides, equations reducible to
  linear form, word problems.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different pedagogical angle):
  [`t2.linear-equations-one-var`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)
  treats linear equations as an algebraic structure; this chapter
  emphasises the balance metaphor and word-problem translation.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**, which remains the canonical
  source for the board paper.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** "Solve for $x$" is the smallest possible mathematical
problem, but the *technique* — apply inverse operations until the
unknown is isolated — is the seed of the entire field of solving
equations, from quadratics in Class 10 to differential equations in
graduate analysis.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Training a neural network is, at its heart, solving
millions of equations of the form *gradient = 0* simultaneously.
Gradient descent is the iterative cousin of "do the same thing to both
sides".
:::
